text: Hello, I'm sharing a robotic correction of a chronic rectal stump leak, which has a fistulous connection to the left tube and ovary causing a secondary TOA. This is a patient who unfortunately suffered a leak after a sigmoid resection, a very complicated sigmoid resection about five months earlier. She underwent a Hartman procedure with closure of rectal stump, suture closure of rectal stump. Unfortunately, that persistently leaked. She had a drain for a while. It appeared that it had closed. The drain was removed. And then another month went by and she presented with a large left-sided tubal ovarian abscess with clear fistula miscommunication to the rectal stump. I did attempt to have my advanced endoscopist close this with a bear claw, which did not work. And so because of the ongoing symptoms and the fact that this was going to delay eventual heartman closure, I elected to take her to the operating room for repair.

The surgeon carefully dissects small bowel adhesions in the pelvis using laparoscopic techniques, aiming to free the loops of small bowel from the pelvic structures without causing additional injuries. The adhesions are notably present at the dome of the bladder, where a previous bladder fistula was located.

The surgeon uses a suction device to assist in dissecting adherent loops of small bowel from the pelvic area, carefully avoiding inadvertent injuries. One loop is successfully freed, and the surgeon addresses oozing by packing with sponges and occasionally using Surgicel to maintain a clear view.

The surgeon works in the retroperitoneal plane to mobilize and free loops of small bowel adherent to the bladder. On the right, a large hydrosalpinx is ruptured and drained. The surgeon unroofs a blind cavity, performing a rectal leak test and methylene blue test to confirm it is not connected to the rectum or bladder. The right Annexa is dissected off the midline, revealing pus from a hole in the rectal stump.

The surgeon successfully frees the right adnexa from surrounding tissue, revealing the rectal mesentery in the midline. A hole in the rectal stump is identified, and a leak test is performed, visibly showing air spurting out. The surgeon then begins working on the left adnexa, which contains a large abscess.

The surgeon uses blunt dissection to navigate the surgical planes around the left tubal ovarian abscess (TOA). Sponges and Surgicel are applied to manage bleeding. The rectal stump lumen is visible, with pus oozing from the left adnexa where it is fistulized to the rectal stump leak. The surgeon compresses the abscess to drain the pus completely.

The surgeon uses blunt dissection to completely free the adnexa, ensuring no trapped collections or fistulous communications to the rectum remain. A larger hole is created into the abscess cavity for complete drainage. Both adnexa are now separated from the midline structure, identified as the rectum. The surgeon considers whether to staple or suture the area closed, with the uterus visible in the field.

The surgeon sutures the rectal stump closed, mobilizing the edges for a secure closure. A three OV lock suture is used for the initial layer, followed by a Proline suture to reinforce the closure. The rectal stump is tested for leaks, which are negative, and a pelvic drain is placed.
